abstract:
In this study, we investigate the properties of nitrogen centers acting as hydrogen-bond or Brønsted acceptors in 17 solid molecular acid-base complexes. Using N 1s X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and $^{15}$N solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (ssNMR) spectroscopy, we interpret these electronic environments with reference to local crystallographic data obtained via X-ray diffraction (XRD). 

Our findings confirm that nitrogen protonation induces a substantial chemical shift in N 1s binding energy, providing an unequivocal method to distinguish between protonated species (salts) and hydrogen-bonded species (co-crystals). This differentiation is further validated by significant ssNMR shifts to low frequency upon proton transfer from the acid to the base. In contrast, co-crystal formation generally results in only minor chemical shifts, except in instances where a particularly strong hydrogen bond is established. 

To bridge experimental and theoretical observations, we utilized CASTEP density functional theory (DFT) to calculate $^{15}$N ssNMR isotropic chemical shifts. The strong correlation between these calculations and our experimental data confirms that computational predictions of H-bond strength and NMR parameters can reliably identify salt and co-crystal structures—an approach central to NMR crystallography. The high level of agreement between XPS and collective CASTEP/ssNMR results offers a robust pathway for characterizing local structures in molecular systems where long-range order is absent, such as non-crystalline or amorphous materials. Given the broad range of systems examined, this integrated spectroscopic approach is highly versatile and applicable to diverse molecular materials across organic, physical, and materials chemistry.
